channel.title: NPR Topics: Health
channel.copyright: Copyright 2024 NPR - For Personal Use Only

item.title: Get into med school in the graphic memoir 'See One, Do One, Teach One'
item.description: Grace Farris' book brings readers behind the med school scenes — documenting anxiety over loans, bouts of impostor syndrome, friendships forged in stress, and the ever-impossible work-life balance.
item.content:encoded: Grace Farris' book brings readers behind the med school scenes — documenting anxiety over loans, bouts of impostor syndrome, friendships forged in stress, and the ever-impossible work-life balance.
item.dc:creator: Tahneer Oksman

item.title: This AI tool promises a 'second pair of eyes' to clinicians. Did patients benefit?
item.description: Medical workers in a Nairobi clinic used an AI tool to check their work. A new study evaluates the result.
item.content:encoded:
Medical workers in a Nairobi clinic used an AI tool to check their work. A new study evaluates the result.

(Image credit: Patrick Meinhardt/AFP)
item.dc:creator: Joseph Kim

item.title: Why health officials do not have a good handle on the current Ebola outbreak
item.description: To get slightly ahead of the virus, health officials need to quickly identify people at risk of infection, a practice known as contact tracing. That's a challenge in the Democratic Republic of Congo.
item.content:encoded:
To get slightly ahead of the virus, health officials need to quickly identify people at risk of infection, a practice known as contact tracing. That's a challenge in the Democratic Republic of Congo.

(Image credit: Michel Lunanga)
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Lambert

item.title: Number of cyclosporiasis cases in the U.S. has surpassed previous records, CDC says
item.description: It can take weeks to confirm a single case of intestinal illness caused by the cyclospora parasite, but the CDC's tally for this year now stands at over 4,100.
item.content:encoded: It can take weeks to confirm a single case of intestinal illness caused by the cyclospora parasite, but the CDC's tally for this year now stands at over 4,100.
item.dc:creator: Yuki Noguchi

item.title: FDA panel considers easing peptide restrictions
item.description: Advisers to the Food and Drug Administration are weighing the evidence for seven different peptides. Favorable votes would pave the way for easier access.
item.content:encoded:
Advisers to the Food and Drug Administration are weighing the evidence for seven different peptides. Favorable votes would pave the way for easier access.

(Image credit: Sarah Silbiger)
item.dc:creator: Will Stone

item.title: Ebola death toll in DR Congo surpasses 1,000
item.description: Congo's Ebola outbreak has crossed the grim 1,000-death threshold, marking the fastest fatal surge in recorded history of the virus.
item.content:encoded:
Congo's Ebola outbreak has crossed the grim 1,000-death threshold, marking the fastest fatal surge in recorded history of the virus.

(Image credit: Moses Sawasawa)
item.dc:creator: Emmet Livingstone

item.title: African countries are facing rising rates of cancer. What's being done about it?
item.description: Cancer diagnoses are on the rise in sub-Saharan Africa and the Eastern Mediterranean regions, but care lags behind.
item.content:encoded: Cancer diagnoses are on the rise in sub-Saharan Africa and the Eastern Mediterranean regions, but care lags behind.
item.dc:creator: Durrie Bouscaren

item.title: A strange transmissible cancer is spreading through the catfish in this lake
item.description: For more than a decade, there's been something fishy going on in a lake that straddles Vermont and the province of Quebec. It involves cancer, a kind of catfish, and — possibly — clues about how tumors metastasize.
item.content:encoded:
For more than a decade, there's been something fishy going on in a lake that straddles Vermont and the province of Quebec. It involves cancer, a kind of catfish, and — possibly — clues about how tumors metastasize.

(Image credit: Joshua Brown)
item.dc:creator: Ari Daniel

item.title: In pursuit of 'Instagram face,' are we losing the imperfections that make us human?
item.description: Plastic surgery is becoming so normalized and undetectable, it's changing our relationship to reality. The New Yorker staff writer Jia Tolentino considers how beauty standards have dovetailed with AI.
item.content:encoded: Plastic surgery is becoming so normalized and undetectable, it's changing our relationship to reality. The New Yorker staff writer Jia Tolentino considers how beauty standards have dovetailed with AI.
item.dc:creator: Tonya Mosley

item.title: Peptides get their regulatory close-up
item.description: A panel of advisers to the Food and Drug Administration will meet this week to make recommendations on whether specialized pharmacies should be allowed to make some popular wellness peptides.
item.content:encoded:
A panel of advisers to the Food and Drug Administration will meet this week to make recommendations on whether specialized pharmacies should be allowed to make some popular wellness peptides.

(Image credit: Javier Arciga)
item.dc:creator: Will Stone
